title: Time & Space Complexity
summary: Big-O notation, complexity hierarchy, common data structure operations, and practical performance thinking.
guide:
# Time & Space Complexity

## Why Care About Complexity?

Your code might run on 10 items in your test, but production might have 10 million.
How do you know if your algorithm will still finish in seconds — or will hang for hours?

**Big-O notation** is a shorthand for: 'as input size grows, how does running time grow?'

```
  Items (n)     O(1)    O(log n)   O(n)      O(n log n)   O(n^2)         O(2^n)
  10            1       3          10        33           100            1024
  100           1       7          100       664          10,000         huge
  1,000         1       10         1,000     9,966        1,000,000      crash
  1,000,000     1       20         1M        20M          1 trillion!    impossible

  Notice: O(n^2) at 1M items = 1 trillion operations = ~1000 SECONDS!
          O(n)   at 1M items = 1M operations         = ~1 millisecond.
  Same task, 1 million times difference in performance.
```

---

## Big-O — The Growth Rate Hierarchy

Big-O describes **the worst-case upper bound** of growth, ignoring constants and small terms.

```
From fastest growing (slowest) to slowest growing (fastest):

O(1)        = constant         — independent of n
                                  array[i], hash get, push to stack

O(log n)    = logarithmic      — halves the problem each step
                                  binary search, balanced BST lookup

O(n)        = linear           — touches each element once
                                  loop through array, max value

O(n log n)  = linearithmic     — divide-and-conquer sorting
                                  merge sort, quick sort (avg)

O(n^2)      = quadratic        — nested loops over n
                                  bubble sort, comparing all pairs

O(n^3)      = cubic            — triple-nested
                                  naive matrix multiply

O(2^n)      = exponential      — recursive branching
                                  naive Fibonacci, subset generation

O(n!)       = factorial        — permutations
                                  brute-force traveling salesman
```

---

## How to Calculate Big-O — Step by Step

```java
// Example 1:
void example1(int n) {
    System.out.println('hi');     // 1 operation
    System.out.println('hi');     // 1 operation
}
// Total: 2 operations, regardless of n
// Big-O: O(1) — drop the constant 2

// Example 2:
void example2(int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {  // runs n times
        System.out.println(i);      //   1 op per iteration
    }
}
// Total: n × 1 = n operations
// Big-O: O(n)

// Example 3:
void example3(int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {        // n times
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {    //   n times
            System.out.println(i + j);   //     1 op
        }
    }
}
// Total: n × n × 1 = n^2 operations
// Big-O: O(n^2)

// Example 4 (TRICKY!):
void example4(int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {        // n times
        System.out.println(i);            //   1 op
    }
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {        // n times
        System.out.println(j);            //   1 op
    }
}
// Total: n + n = 2n operations
// Big-O: O(n) — drop the constant 2
// Sequential loops ADD; nested loops MULTIPLY.
```

---

## Rules of Thumb

```
1. DROP CONSTANTS:
   O(2n) → O(n)
   O(500) → O(1)
   O(n/2) → O(n)

2. DROP NON-DOMINANT TERMS:
   O(n^2 + n) → O(n^2)
   O(n + log n) → O(n)
   O(2^n + 1000n) → O(2^n)

3. DIFFERENT INPUTS = DIFFERENT VARIABLES:
   for (int i = 0; i < a; i++) {
     for (int j = 0; j < b; j++) {  ← b is independent of a
       ...
     }
   }
   Big-O: O(a × b)  NOT  O(n^2)

4. RECURSIVE BRANCHING IS EXPONENTIAL:
   fib(n) calls fib(n-1) and fib(n-2) — branching factor 2
   Big-O: O(2^n)

5. LOG SHOWS UP WHEN YOU HALVE:
   Binary search: halve search space each step
   Tree traversal: balanced tree height = log n
   How many times can n be halved before reaching 1? log_2(n) times.
```

---

## Common Operations Cheat Sheet

```
ARRAY:
  Access by index (arr[i]):    O(1)
  Search for value:            O(n)
  Insert at end (amortised):   O(1)
  Insert at front:             O(n) — shifts everything
  Sort:                        O(n log n)

ARRAYLIST / DYNAMIC ARRAY (Java's ArrayList):
  Same as array, but resize on full = amortised O(1) for append

LINKED LIST:
  Access by index:             O(n) — must traverse
  Insert/delete at head/tail:  O(1) — if you have the reference
  Insert in middle:            O(n) — find then O(1) link

HASH MAP / HASH SET:
  Get/Put/Contains (avg):      O(1)
  Get/Put/Contains (worst):    O(n) — when many collisions
  Iterate all keys:            O(n)

BINARY SEARCH TREE (balanced):
  Insert/Search/Delete:        O(log n)
  Worst case (unbalanced):     O(n) — like a linked list

HEAP / PRIORITY QUEUE:
  Insert:                      O(log n)
  Peek min/max:                O(1)
  Extract min/max:             O(log n)

STACK / QUEUE (LinkedList-backed):
  Push/Pop/Enqueue/Dequeue:    O(1)

SORTING:
  Quicksort (avg):             O(n log n)
  Quicksort (worst):           O(n^2) — already sorted with bad pivot
  Mergesort:                   O(n log n) — guaranteed
  Bubble/Insertion/Selection:  O(n^2)

GRAPH:
  BFS/DFS (V vertices, E edges): O(V + E)
  Dijkstra with heap:          O((V + E) log V)
```

---

## Space Complexity — Memory Usage

```
Space complexity = how much extra memory does the algorithm use
(not counting the input itself).

void sumOf(int[] arr) {                    // input: arr
    int sum = 0;                            // O(1) extra space
    for (int x : arr) sum += x;
}
// Space: O(1)

int[] doubleArray(int[] arr) {              // input: arr
    int[] result = new int[arr.length];     // O(n) extra space
    for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
        result[i] = arr[i] * 2;
    }
    return result;
}
// Space: O(n)

// RECURSION USES STACK SPACE
int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}
// Space: O(n) — call stack depth, even though branching is 2^n in time
// Each recursive call adds a stack frame (function arguments, locals).
// Stack overflow when depth exceeds ~1MB (~10000 frames in Java).
```

---

## Real-World Mental Calibration

```
ROUGH RULE: A modern CPU does ~10^8 simple operations per second.

If n = 10^6 (1 million):
  O(n)        = 10^6 ops    = ~10ms      ✓ instant
  O(n log n)  = 2×10^7 ops  = ~200ms     ✓ acceptable
  O(n^2)      = 10^12 ops   = ~3 hours   ✗ too slow

If n = 10^9 (1 billion):
  O(n)        = 10^9 ops    = ~10s       ✓ slow but ok for batch
  O(n log n)  = 3×10^10 ops = ~5 min    ✓ tolerable
  O(n^2)      = 10^18 ops   = 30+ years  ✗ never

PRACTICAL THRESHOLDS (for online API or interactive UI):
  n ≤ 10:           any algorithm works (even O(n!) for n ≤ 8)
  n ≤ 100:          O(n^3) ok
  n ≤ 1,000:        O(n^2) ok
  n ≤ 1,000,000:    O(n log n) needed
  n ≤ 10,000,000:   O(n) needed
  n > 10^9:         O(log n) or pre-computed

INTERVIEW HEURISTIC: if your O(n^2) solution looks reasonable,
the interviewer almost certainly wants you to find an O(n log n) or O(n) approach.
```

---

## Mind Map

```
TIME & SPACE COMPLEXITY
|
+-- BIG-O NOTATION
|   +-- Worst-case upper bound
|   +-- Drop constants, drop non-dominants
|   +-- Sequential adds, nested multiplies
|
+-- HIERARCHY (best to worst)
|   +-- O(1)        constant
|   +-- O(log n)    halve each step
|   +-- O(n)        single pass
|   +-- O(n log n)  divide-and-conquer sort
|   +-- O(n^2)      nested loops
|   +-- O(2^n)      recursive branching
|   +-- O(n!)       permutations
|
+-- DATA STRUCTURE OPS
|   +-- Array: O(1) access, O(n) search
|   +-- HashMap: O(1) avg, O(n) worst
|   +-- BST balanced: O(log n) all ops
|   +-- Heap: O(log n) insert/extract
|
+-- SPACE COMPLEXITY
    +-- Auxiliary memory used by algorithm
    +-- Recursion = O(depth) stack frames
    +-- Tradeoff: space for time (memoisation)
```

---

## How This Connects to Other Topics

- **DSA**: every algorithm and data structure topic uses Big-O analysis
- **System Design**: 'will this scale to 1M users?' = Big-O thinking on a system level
- **Database queries**: SQL EXPLAIN shows query complexity (full scan = O(n), index = O(log n))
- **Frontend rendering**: O(n^2) re-renders kill React performance — same Big-O thinking

---

questions.prompt: What does Big-O notation describe?
choices: An upper bound on growth rate as input size approaches infinity, ignoring constants and lower-order terms
The memory layout of an algorithm
questions.explanation: Big-O is asymptotic notation. It describes how an algorithm's runtime/memory grows as n grows large, ignoring constants (5n vs 1n both = O(n)) and lower-order terms (n^2 + n = O(n^2)). It's a worst-case upper bound — the algorithm performs no worse than this growth rate.

questions.prompt: Two sequential O(n) loops over the same input — what's the total complexity?
choices: O(2n) which simplifies to O(n) — sequential loops add, then we drop constants
questions.explanation: Sequential operations ADD. n + n = 2n. Big-O drops constants: O(2n) = O(n). Compare to NESTED loops which multiply: O(n×n) = O(n^2). Recognising sequential vs nested is the most common Big-O mistake.

questions.prompt: Binary search on a sorted array of n elements — what's the complexity?
choices: O(log n) — each step halves the search space, so the number of steps is log_2(n)
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the remaining search range each comparison. Starting at n, the question is: how many times can you halve n before reaching 1? Answer: log_2(n) times. For n=1M, that's only ~20 comparisons. This is why sorted arrays + binary search beat linear search dramatically.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of accessing arr[i] in a Java array?
choices: O(1) — array access by index uses pointer arithmetic: base_address + (i × element_size). Single memory read regardless of array size or position.
questions.explanation: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory. arr[i] = base + i*sizeof(element). Computed in constant time, single memory access. Linked list arr[i] is O(n) because you must walk node by node. This is why arrays are preferred for random access.

questions.prompt: What is the WORST-case time complexity of HashMap.get(key) in Java?
choices: O(n) — when many keys hash to the same bucket (collisions), lookup degrades to a linear scan of that bucket. Java 8+ converts long collision chains to a tree (O(log n)) at threshold.
questions.explanation: HashMap average-case: O(1). Worst-case: O(n) when all keys hash to same bucket. Java 8+ improvement: when bucket has 8+ entries, converts to red-black tree, making worst-case O(log n). Most code only cares about average. Bad hash function or adversarial input can trigger worst case.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of this Java code?
choices: O(n^2) — nested loops over same n
O(a × b) where a and b are different sizes
questions.explanation: Nested loops multiply, but a and b are DIFFERENT inputs. Don't lazily call this O(n^2) — that assumes a.length == b.length. Correct: O(a × b). If a=1000 and b=10, it's 10,000 operations, not 1,000,000. Different inputs need different variables in Big-O analysis.

questions.prompt: What is the space complexity of recursive Fibonacci: fib(n) returns fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)?
choices: O(n) — call stack depth is at most n, even though TIME complexity is O(2^n) due to recursive branching
questions.explanation: Subtle: time vs space differ. Time: each call branches into 2, so total calls is O(2^n). Space: at any moment, only one branch is being computed; max stack depth = n (going from fib(n) down to fib(0)). Once fib(n-1) returns, that stack space is reclaimed before fib(n-2) is called.

questions.prompt: O(n^2 + 1000n + 5) simplifies to:
choices: O(n^2) — drop non-dominant terms (1000n is dwarfed by n^2 for large n) and constants
questions.explanation: Drop non-dominant terms: as n grows, n^2 dwarfs 1000n. At n=10000: n^2 = 10^8, 1000n = 10^7 — the n^2 term is 10x bigger. At n=10^6: n^2 = 10^12 vs 1000n = 10^9 — 1000x. Constants and lower-order terms become irrelevant. Final: O(n^2).

questions.prompt: For an input of n=10^6, which complexity is too slow for a typical interactive web request (target <100ms)?
questions.explanation: Mental model: ~10^8 simple ops/sec on a modern CPU. n=10^6: O(n)=10^6=10ms (fine), O(n log n)=2×10^7=200ms (borderline), O(n^2)=10^12=10000s (impossible). Online APIs need O(n) or better at n=10^6. O(n^2) is fine only when n ≤ ~3000.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of mergesort?
choices: O(n log n) — guaranteed in ALL cases (best, average, worst). Divide step has log n levels, each level processes n elements.
questions.explanation: Mergesort divides the array log n times (each step halves), and at each level merges n elements total. Result: n × log n = O(n log n). Unlike quicksort (which can degrade to O(n^2) on bad pivots), mergesort guarantees O(n log n) in worst case. Trade-off: O(n) extra space.

questions.prompt: Inserting at the FRONT of an ArrayList of size n — complexity?
choices: O(n) — must shift all existing elements one position to the right
questions.explanation: ArrayList stores elements in a backing array. Inserting at front: every existing element shifts right by 1 = n shifts = O(n). Inserting at END is O(1) amortised (occasional resize). For frequent front inserts: use ArrayDeque or LinkedList instead.

questions.prompt: In a balanced binary search tree (Red-Black, AVL), search complexity is:
choices: O(log n) — tree height is bounded at log_2(n) due to balancing rotations
questions.explanation: Balanced BSTs maintain height ~log n via rotations. Each search descends one level per comparison. Worst case: log_2(n) comparisons. For n=1M: ~20 comparisons. Java TreeMap uses red-black tree. Unbalanced BST (insert sorted data) degenerates to linked list = O(n) — that's why balancing matters.

questions.prompt: What does 'amortised O(1)' mean for ArrayList.add() at end?
choices: Average over a long sequence of operations: most adds are O(1), but occasional resizes cost O(n). Total cost / number of operations = O(1) on average.
questions.explanation: ArrayList grows by doubling when full. Most adds: O(1). When full, resize: copy n elements to new array = O(n) for that one add. But resizes are rare: after n adds, only log(n) resizes happened. Total: n + 2 + 4 + ... + n = ~3n operations for n adds. Per add: 3n/n = 3 = O(1) amortised.

questions.prompt: Which algorithm is correctly classified?
choices: Quick sort average: O(n log n) — typical good-pivot case
questions.explanation: Quicksort: average O(n log n), worst O(n^2). Bubble sort: O(n^2). Hash insert avg: O(1). Linear search: O(n). Knowing the standard complexities is essential for technical interviews and choosing the right algorithm for a problem.

questions.prompt: Time complexity of generating all subsets of n elements:
choices: O(2^n) — there are 2^n subsets (each element either in or out), and we must produce each
questions.explanation: For n elements, there are 2^n possible subsets (each element has 2 choices: included/excluded). Even just enumerating them is O(2^n). For n=20: 1M subsets. For n=30: 1 billion. n=40: 1 trillion (intractable). This is why subset/power-set algorithms work only for small n.

questions.prompt: Memoisation vs naive recursion — typical impact on Fibonacci?
choices: Time O(2^n) → O(n), space O(n) → O(n) — memo cache trades space for huge time savings
questions.explanation: Naive fib(n) recomputes fib(5) hundreds of times in fib(40). Memoisation: cache results. Each fib(k) computed exactly once. Time goes from O(2^n) to O(n). Space increases from O(n) call stack to O(n) call stack + O(n) cache = still O(n). Classic 'space for time' trade.

questions.prompt: Which operations are O(1) on average for HashMap?
questions.explanation: get/put/containsKey/remove are all O(1) average (worst O(n) on collisions). keySet() returns a view but iterating it is O(n). Just calling keySet() is O(1) (returns lazy view), but iterating O(n).

questions.prompt: What is O(n) space complexity caused by in this code?
choices: Creating a new array of size n inside the function — auxiliary space, separate from input
questions.explanation: Space complexity counts EXTRA memory beyond input. A new array allocates n elements = O(n) auxiliary space. Loop variables and print statements use O(1). The input array doesn't count (we already had it). To reduce: try to modify in-place if possible.

questions.prompt: Why is binary search O(log n) and not O(n/2)?
choices: O(n/2) simplifies to O(n) (drop constants), and binary search isn't 'half the work' — it RECURSIVELY halves until reaching 1, doing log_2(n) comparisons total.
questions.explanation: Common confusion. n/2 = doing half the work once = still linear (Big-O drops constants). Binary search keeps halving the remaining range: n → n/2 → n/4 → n/8 → ... → 1. Number of halvings to go from n to 1 = log_2(n). That's why it's O(log n), not O(n/2).

questions.prompt: For Heap (PriorityQueue) in Java, which operation is O(1)?
choices: peek (look at min) — accessing root of heap is constant time
questions.explanation: Min-heap: smallest element is always at root (index 0 of array-backed heap). peek() returns it in O(1). offer() and poll() require sift-up/sift-down to maintain heap property, both O(log n). contains() requires linear scan O(n) — heaps aren't designed for searching arbitrary values.

flashcards.back: 1) Drop constants: O(2n)=O(n), O(500)=O(1). 2) Drop non-dominant terms: O(n^2+n)=O(n^2). 3) Sequential ops add, nested ops multiply. 4) Different inputs = different variables: O(a × b) not O(n^2).
O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!). At n=10^6: O(n)=10ms, O(n log n)=200ms, O(n^2)=hours, O(2^n)=impossible. Choose algorithm based on expected n.
Array: O(1) access by index, O(n) search. LinkedList: O(n) access, O(1) head/tail insert. HashMap: O(1) avg get/put/contains. Pick by: random access frequency, insert position, lookup pattern.

Balanced BST (Red-Black, AVL): O(log n) all ops, height bounded. Unbalanced (insert sorted): degenerates to linked list, O(n). Java TreeMap is red-black. C++ std::map too. Manually implementing BST without rotations = bug.

Quicksort: avg O(n log n), worst O(n^2). Mergesort: O(n log n) guaranteed, O(n) extra space. Heapsort: O(n log n) guaranteed, O(1) space. Insertion: O(n^2), but O(n) on nearly-sorted. Bubble: never use (O(n^2)).

Each recursive call adds a stack frame (locals, return address). Depth d = O(d) space. Java default stack: ~1MB ≈ 10K frames. Too deep = StackOverflowError. Convert to iteration with explicit stack, or use tail-call optimisation (not Java).

Cache previously computed results. Naive fib(n)=O(2^n). Memoised fib(n)=O(n) time, O(n) space. Apply when: 1) function deterministic, 2) overlapping subproblems, 3) memory available. Map<Input, Output> in instance variable.
For interactive (<100ms): n<10⁶ → need O(n log n). n<10⁹ → need O(n) or pre-compute. n<10¹² → need O(log n) or external. n>10¹² → distributed/streaming. Match algorithm complexity to expected n!

project.brief: You're optimising a feature that ranks 100,000 search results by relevance. The naive implementation uses a nested O(n^2) loop that's taking 8 seconds. Design a faster approach: 1) Identify what makes the current code O(n^2). 2) Propose 3 alternative approaches with their complexities. 3) Pick the best for this use case. 4) Estimate runtime improvement.

checklist.text: Identify the O(n²) cause: probably nested comparison or score calculation. State it precisely.
Approach 1: pre-compute scores once O(n), then sort O(n log n) — total O(n log n)
Approach 2: heap of top-K results — O(n log k) where k=20 results shown
Approach 3: parallelise across cores — same Big-O but constant speedup proportional to cores
Estimate: O(n²)=10^10 ops≈100s. O(n log n)=2×10^6 ops≈20ms. ~5000x speedup. Best choice: pre-compute + sort.

hints: If only top 20 results displayed: O(n log k) heap approach has the smallest k factor — fastest in practice for top-K.
Be careful: if scores need to be recomputed per query (depend on query terms), pre-compute may not apply. Then heap of top-K is optimal.
Constants matter: O(n log n) sort with cache-friendly contiguous memory is often faster than O(n) algorithms that pointer-chase.
